Walk through containerizing and deploying a FastAPI RAG service on AWS ECS Fargate with a CI/CD pipeline. What are the moving parts?

The moving parts, in the order a request meets them and in the order a deploy flows:

The app: a FastAPI service exposing /chat, /upload, /health — stateless (sessions in Redis/ElastiCache, files in S3, vectors in a managed vector DB or OpenSearch, metadata in RDS Postgres). Dockerize it: slim Python base image, dependencies layer cached before code copy, non-root user, uvicorn entrypoint; secrets *never* baked in — injected at runtime from AWS Secrets Manager/SSM.

The runtime: push the image to ECR (AWS's registry). An ECS task definition declares the container: image URI, CPU/memory, environment, secrets references, log configuration (awslogs → CloudWatch). An ECS service on Fargate keeps N tasks running — Fargate means no EC2 to patch; you rent CPU/RAM per task. An Application Load Balancer fronts the service: TLS termination (ACM certificate), health checks against /health (failing tasks get replaced automatically), and support for streaming responses. Auto scaling policies scale task count on CPU or request-count-per-target; networking lives in a VPC with the ALB public and tasks in private subnets.

The pipeline (GitHub Actions or CodePipeline), on every merge to main: run tests + lint → run the *RAG evaluation harness* (the GenAI-specific step interviewers should hear: golden Q&A set scored for faithfulness/relevance — a quality gate, not just unit tests) → build image, tag with git SHA → push to ECR → update task definition revision → trigger ECS rolling deployment (new tasks start, pass health checks, old tasks drain — zero downtime; blue/green via CodeDeploy if you want instant rollback) → smoke test the live /health and a canary query.

Observability closes the loop: CloudWatch logs/metrics/alarms (p95 latency, 5xx, task restarts), plus LLM-level tracing (Langfuse/LangSmith) and cost dashboards. Soundbite: 'ECR holds the artifact, task definition describes it, Fargate runs it, ALB fronts it, the pipeline gates on tests *and* eval scores, and rolling deploys make releases boring — which is the goal.'

Hugging Face vs LangChain: why do people say one is a 'training mindset' and the other an 'orchestration mindset'?

Because they operate on different layers of the stack, and confusing the layers is a classic beginner tell.

Hugging Face is the *model layer* — the ecosystem for the models themselves: the Hub (hundreds of thousands of weights, datasets, tokenizers), Transformers (load and run models), PEFT (LoRA/QLoRA adapters), TRL (SFT, DPO, RLHF trainers), Accelerate (multi-GPU), and Datasets. You live here when you *change or serve weights*: fine-tuning Qwen2.5-1.5B on your instruction dataset, quantizing it, publishing a checkpoint, evaluating perplexity. The mindset is: data → training loop → weights → inference. Your entire fine-tuning project — SFT on the instruction file, DPO on the preference file — is a Hugging Face-stack project (Transformers + PEFT + TRL, or Unsloth on top for speed).

LangChain is the *application layer* — orchestration of calls to models (whoever serves them: OpenAI, Bedrock, or your own endpoint): prompt templates, chains/LCEL pipelines, retrievers and vector-store integrations for RAG, tool/agent abstractions, memory, and output parsers; LangGraph for stateful agent graphs; LangSmith for tracing. You live here when you *compose systems around frozen models*: a RAG pipeline, a router, a multi-agent workflow. The mindset is: prompt → model call → parse → next step.

They meet in the middle: fine-tune with HF, serve the model (TGI/vLLM/SageMaker), then LangChain treats your custom model as just another chat-model endpoint inside a larger RAG/agent app. The interview-ready formulation: 'Hugging Face changes what a model *is*; LangChain decides what a model *does* in a system. Training mindset optimizes weights against a loss; orchestration mindset optimizes flows around a frozen model — real products almost always need a little of the first and a lot of the second.'

You've fine-tuned Qwen2.5-1.5B with SFT and then DPO. How do you prove the DPO stage actually helped — and what failure smells tell you it hurt?

Proving alignment gains is subtler than proving SFT gains, because DPO changes *preferences and style*, not raw knowledge — plain loss curves won't tell the story.

The proof stack: (1) Held-out pairwise win-rate — the headline metric. On an evaluation set of prompts *never seen in training*, generate answers from the SFT-only checkpoint and the SFT+DPO checkpoint, then have an LLM judge (a strong model from a *different* family, both orders evaluated to kill position bias) pick the better answer per your rubric — clarity, depth, groundedness, helpfulness. DPO helped if it wins meaningfully above 50%; report the margin and sample size. Spot-check a slice with human judgment to calibrate the judge. (2) Capability regression suite — alignment must not tax knowledge: re-run your factual/domain Q&A checks and any standard benchmarks you care about, comparing SFT vs DPO checkpoints. A quality-preference gain that costs correctness is a net loss. (3) Training-signal sanity — during DPO, monitor reward margin (implicit logit gap between chosen and rejected) rising, and rejected-response probability falling while chosen stays healthy. (4) Format/behavior checks — refusal correctness, structure compliance, verbosity distribution before/after.

Failure smells: verbosity inflation — the model got longer, not better (a known DPO artifact when 'chosen' answers were systematically longer; your judge must not reward length); reward hacking on style — confident tone up, factual accuracy flat or down; mode collapse / repetition — outputs converge to formulaic templates, diversity dies (often beta too low or too many epochs on a small pair set); catastrophic drift — the model forgets instructions or degrades on off-distribution prompts (KL to the reference model too loose); and preference-set overfitting — huge win-rate on training-like prompts, ~50% on fresh ones.

Interview compression: 'win-rate on held-out prompts with a debiased judge, minus any capability regression, is the proof; longer-but-not-better, samey outputs, and off-distribution drift are the three smells that DPO optimized the letter of your preferences instead of the spirit.'

Why do transformers need positional encoding at all if self-attention already looks at every token?

Self-attention is permutation-invariant by design - it computes weighted sums over all tokens regardless of their order, so 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would produce identical attention patterns without help. Positional encoding injects the missing order information directly into the token embeddings before they hit the attention layers.

The original Transformer used fixed sinusoidal encodings - each position gets a unique pattern of sine and cosine waves at different frequencies, added to the embedding. Modern models mostly use RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding), which instead rotates the Query and Key vectors by an angle proportional to their position. RoPE's trick is that the dot product between a rotated Query and Key naturally encodes *relative* distance, not just absolute position - which generalizes much better to sequences longer than anything seen in training.

Interview takeaway: absolute encodings tell the model 'you are at position 5'; relative encodings like RoPE tell it 'you are 3 tokens after that one' - and relative position is what actually matters for language, which is why RoPE dominates modern LLMs (Llama, Qwen, Mistral).

The self-attention formula is O(n^2) in sequence length. Why does that matter, and how do modern models get around it?

Every token attends to every other token, so doubling the context length quadruples the compute and memory for the attention matrix. A 4K context is manageable; a 128K context with naive attention would need a matrix with 16 billion entries per head - it simply does not fit in GPU memory.

Two classes of fixes exist. The first is algorithmic: FlashAttention doesn't reduce the O(n^2) math, but it restructures the computation to avoid ever materializing the full attention matrix in slow GPU memory, streaming it in tiles through fast SRAM instead - same result, far less memory traffic, which is why it gives a 2-4x speedup for free. The second is architectural: sliding-window attention (Mistral), grouped local+global attention, or state-space alternatives (Mamba) cap how many tokens each token can actually attend to, trading some long-range recall for linear scaling.

Interview takeaway: FlashAttention is an I/O optimization that computes the exact same attention faster; sliding-window attention is an approximation that changes what the model can see. Production long-context models usually combine both.

What is the KV cache, and why does inference get faster after the first token but training doesn't use it at all?

During autoregressive generation, each new token needs to attend to every previous token's Key and Value vectors. Without caching, you'd recompute K and V for the entire prefix on every single new token - wildly wasteful, since those vectors never change once computed. The KV cache stores them after the first forward pass, so generating token 501 only requires computing K/V for token 500 and reusing the cached 499 before it.

This is why time-to-first-token and time-per-subsequent-token behave so differently in production: the first token pays the full prefill cost, but every token after is cheap - just one new row of attention against a growing cache. It's also why long conversations get memory-hungry: the cache grows linearly with context length and must be held per active request, which is the real reason serving frameworks like vLLM obsess over KV cache memory management (PagedAttention).

Training doesn't use a KV cache because it processes the whole sequence in parallel with a causal mask in one forward pass - there's no token-by-token loop to cache across.

Interview takeaway: KV cache trades memory for compute during generation; it's irrelevant during training because training isn't sequential.

Greedy decoding, beam search, top-k, and top-p (nucleus) sampling all pick the next token differently. When would you use each?

Greedy decoding always picks the single highest-probability token - fast and deterministic, but prone to bland, repetitive text because it never explores alternatives; good for tasks with one clearly correct answer, like extracting a number from text.

Beam search keeps the top-k most probable *sequences* at each step, not just tokens, exploring several paths before committing - it improves quality for tasks like translation where you want the globally best sequence, but it's expensive and can still produce generic, safe-sounding text.

Top-k sampling randomly samples from only the k most likely next tokens, adding controlled randomness while cutting off the long tail of nonsense options. Top-p (nucleus) sampling is the more adaptive cousin: instead of a fixed k, it samples from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p (say 0.9) - so the candidate pool shrinks when the model is confident and grows when it's uncertain.

Interview takeaway: use greedy or low-temperature decoding for factual/structured output, top-p with moderate temperature (0.7-0.9) for creative or conversational output. Beam search is mostly seen in classic seq2seq/translation systems today, not chat LLMs.

What does perplexity actually measure, and why can a model with great perplexity still be a bad chatbot?

Perplexity is, informally, 'how surprised was the model by the actual next token, on average' - mathematically it's the exponential of the average negative log-likelihood the model assigned to the correct tokens in a held-out text. Lower perplexity means the model consistently assigned high probability to what actually came next, i.e. it predicts the training-like distribution well.

The catch: perplexity only measures how well a model predicts *existing* text, not whether its own generated responses are correct, helpful, or safe. A model can have excellent perplexity on Wikipedia because it's great at modeling encyclopedic prose, while still failing to follow instructions, hallucinating facts, or giving unhelpful answers as a chatbot - because being a good next-token predictor and being a good assistant are related but distinct skills, and the second one is exactly what instruction tuning and DPO are for.

Interview takeaway: perplexity is the right metric for judging base/pretrained language modeling quality; for judging an instruction-tuned assistant you need task-based evaluation (correctness, helpfulness, human or LLM-judge preference), not perplexity.

What is Mixture of Experts (MoE), and why do models like Mixtral use it instead of a plain dense transformer?

In a dense transformer, every token passes through every parameter in every feed-forward layer - a 47B dense model spends all 47B parameters' worth of compute on every single token. MoE replaces the single feed-forward block with several parallel 'expert' feed-forward networks and a small router network that picks only the top-k experts (often just 2) to actually process each token.

The result is a model with a huge total parameter count (Mixtral 8x7B has ~47B total parameters) but a much smaller *active* parameter count per token (~13B) - so inference and training compute scale with the active parameters, not the total, while the model still has the representational capacity of the full parameter count spread across specialized experts.

The hard parts are load balancing (making sure the router doesn't collapse to always using the same 2 experts) and the fact that you still need enough VRAM to hold *all* experts even though only a few run per token, so MoE saves compute but not memory the way people sometimes assume.

Interview takeaway: MoE decouples 'how much the model knows' (total parameters) from 'how much compute each token costs' (active parameters) - it's a compute-efficiency trick, not a memory-efficiency trick.

When should you extend the context window and stuff everything in the prompt, versus building a RAG pipeline?

Long context and RAG solve the same surface problem - 'the model needs information it doesn't have in its weights' - but with very different cost and reliability profiles. Long context is simpler: no retrieval pipeline, no chunking decisions, no re-ranking bugs, and the model can reason across the entire document at once. But it's expensive per call (you pay for those tokens every single request), it's slow (bigger prefill), and research consistently shows 'lost in the middle' - models attend less reliably to information buried in the center of a very long context.

RAG is cheaper per call (you only send the few relevant chunks) and scales to corpora far larger than any context window (millions of documents), but it introduces its own failure modes - bad chunking, retrieval missing the right passage, embedding model mismatch with domain vocabulary.

A practical rule: if the knowledge base is small, static, and needed in full for every query (a single 50-page contract you're auditing clause-by-clause), long context wins. If the knowledge base is large, growing, or only a small fraction is relevant per query (a whole company's documentation), RAG wins on cost and scale.

Interview takeaway: long context vs RAG is a cost/latency/scale tradeoff, not an accuracy-only question - many production systems now use both, retrieving coarse candidates then giving the model a long-context window to reason over them.

How does function calling / tool calling actually work under the hood in an LLM API?

The model itself never executes code - function calling works by giving the model, alongside the prompt, a structured schema describing available tools (name, description, and a JSON Schema of parameters). During generation the model is trained to recognize when a tool would help and to emit a special structured output - typically a JSON object naming the function and its arguments - instead of (or alongside) natural language.

The orchestration layer (your application code, not the model) parses that structured output, actually calls the real function or API, and feeds the result back into the conversation as a new message, so the model can read the tool's output and produce a final natural-language answer. The whole loop - prompt with tools -> model emits a call -> your code executes it -> result appended -> model responds - can chain multiple times for multi-step tool use.

Reliability in practice comes from constrained decoding or JSON-mode features that force the output to match the schema grammar, rather than hoping the model gets the syntax right through prompting alone.

Interview takeaway: function calling is really 'structured output plus an execution loop you control' - the model decides *what* to call, your infrastructure decides *how* it actually runs, which is exactly the security boundary you must respect when giving an agent access to real systems.

Why does it matter that the system prompt and user prompt are separate roles instead of just concatenating everything into one block of text?

Role separation exists because instruction-tuned models are trained to treat the system role as a higher-trust, persistent instruction channel and the user role as the (lower-trust, per-turn) content to respond to. This isn't just an API convenience - it's baked into the model's training data, where system instructions consistently take priority over conflicting user requests during fine-tuning and RLHF/DPO.

Practically, this gives you a lever for safety and consistency: you can lock behavior, tone, and constraints in the system prompt ('never reveal internal pricing, always cite sources') with reasonable confidence the model will weight it more heavily than an adversarial instruction buried in user-supplied or retrieved text. It's also why prompt injection is dangerous specifically when untrusted content (a scraped webpage, a user-uploaded document) gets treated as if it were a system or developer instruction rather than plain user-role data - the injected text can now compete for the same trust level as your real instructions.

Interview takeaway: role separation is a training-time trust hierarchy, not just formatting - it's your first (imperfect) line of defense, which is why guardrails must never rely on it alone.

Few-shot prompting means giving the model examples in the prompt. When does that actually help versus just wasting tokens?

Few-shot examples help most when the task has an unusual or ambiguous *format* that the model wouldn't guess from an instruction alone - a specific JSON schema, a particular tone, a domain-specific classification scheme with fine boundaries. Two or three well-chosen examples can pin down exactly what 'correct' looks like far more reliably than describing it in prose.

Few-shot helps less, and can even hurt, when the task is already well within the model's zero-shot competence (basic summarization, common-sense Q&A) - the examples just burn context tokens and money without moving quality, and if the examples are subtly inconsistent or unrepresentative, the model can overfit to spurious patterns in them (e.g. always picking option B because two of your three examples happened to).

As a rule of thumb: for well-instruction-tuned modern models, invest in few-shot examples for format/schema conformance and edge-case calibration, but rely on zero-shot plus a clear instruction for straightforward reasoning or generation tasks - and always check whether fine-tuning (baking the pattern into weights) would be cheaper than paying for examples on every single call at scale.

Interview takeaway: few-shot is a prompt-time patch for format and edge cases; if you're sending the same examples on every request at high volume, that's a signal to fine-tune instead.

You're deciding between prompt engineering, RAG, and fine-tuning for a new LLM feature. How do you actually choose?

Think of it as three levers with increasing cost and decreasing flexibility, and pick the cheapest one that solves the actual problem. Prompt engineering (plus few-shot examples) fixes *behavior and format* problems - the model knows the answer but phrases it wrong, forgets instructions, or needs a specific output structure. It's free to iterate and should always be tried first.

RAG fixes *knowledge* problems - the model doesn't have the facts because they're private, proprietary, or post-training-cutoff. No amount of clever prompting teaches the model your internal HR policy; you have to retrieve it. RAG doesn't change how the model behaves, only what it knows at query time.

Fine-tuning fixes *pattern and style* problems that persist across all inputs and are too expensive or unreliable to re-explain in every single prompt - consistent domain tone, a specialized output schema baked in, or teaching the model to reliably use unusual vocabulary or reasoning patterns it doesn't produce naturally even with good prompts.

Interview takeaway: 'does it know it?' -> RAG. 'Does it do it consistently?' -> fine-tune. 'Am I explaining the same thing every time?' -> prompt engineering has already failed you, move up the stack. Most serious production systems end up combining all three.

What is catastrophic forgetting in fine-tuning, and why is it a bigger risk with full fine-tuning than with LoRA?

Catastrophic forgetting is when training a model on a new, narrow dataset overwrites capabilities it already had - a model fine-tuned heavily on legal contracts might get noticeably worse at casual conversation or basic arithmetic, because the gradient updates needed to specialize on legal text nudge the same weights that encoded those other skills.

Full fine-tuning is riskier because every one of the billions of parameters is free to move, so with a small or narrow dataset (and especially too many epochs or too high a learning rate) the model can drift far from its original, broadly-capable weights. LoRA constrains the damage almost by construction: the base weights are frozen entirely, and only small low-rank update matrices are trained and added on top - the model's original knowledge is structurally preserved, and the adapter can even be removed to instantly restore the exact base behavior.

Mitigations beyond LoRA include mixing in a slice of general-purpose instruction data alongside the domain data (replay), lower learning rates, fewer epochs, and evaluating on general benchmarks - not just domain benchmarks - after every fine-tuning run.

Interview takeaway: LoRA doesn't eliminate forgetting, it bounds it - the frozen base weights are a built-in safety net that full fine-tuning doesn't have.

Alpaca-style datasets use instruction/input/output. ShareGPT-style datasets use a list of chat messages. Why does the format you train on matter?

The dataset format needs to match the chat template and use-case the model will actually be deployed with, because the model learns the *shape* of a conversation turn as much as the content. Alpaca-style (instruction + optional input + single output) is simple and great for single-turn task completion - summarize this, classify that - but a model trained only on that format can behave oddly in multi-turn chat, because it never saw examples of referring back to earlier turns or handling a running conversation.

ShareGPT/chat-style data (a list of {role, content} turns, often multi-turn) trains the model on the actual conversational structure it will face in production - system/user/assistant turns, multi-turn context, sometimes tool calls interleaved - which is why essentially all modern instruction-tuned models (Llama-3-Instruct, Qwen-Instruct) are trained and evaluated in this chat-message format, and why Unsloth/HF tooling expects you to apply a `chat_template` that turns those messages into the exact token sequence (with special role tokens) the base model's tokenizer defines.

Interview takeaway: mismatched format between training data and inference-time prompting is a silent quality killer - always use the target model's official chat template when formatting instruction data, don't hand-rolled your own.

RLHF trains a separate reward model before doing reinforcement learning. What is that reward model, and how is it trained?

The reward model's job is to replace a human rater with a fast, differentiable stand-in for 'how good is this response' so that reinforcement learning can optimize against it without needing a human in the loop for every single update. It's trained on human preference data: annotators are shown a prompt with two (or more) candidate responses and pick which one is better.

Architecturally, the reward model is usually the base LLM itself with the language-modeling head replaced by a single scalar output, trained with a pairwise ranking loss (Bradley-Terry) that pushes the score of the preferred response above the rejected one. Once trained, it can score any new (prompt, response) pair without a human present.

That trained reward model is then used as the reward signal for PPO: the policy model generates a response, the reward model scores it, and PPO nudges the policy to produce higher-scoring responses - while a KL penalty against the original SFT model keeps it from drifting into responses that game the reward model rather than genuinely being better.

Interview takeaway: the reward model turns subjective human preference into a numeric signal RL can optimize - and its quality is the ceiling for how good RLHF can possibly make the final model.

PPO is the classic RLHF algorithm but it's notoriously unstable and expensive. What exactly makes it hard, and how does that motivate DPO?

PPO for RLHF requires juggling four models simultaneously in memory during training: the policy model being trained, a frozen reference model (for the KL penalty), the reward model (to score generations), and often a separate value/critic model (to estimate advantages) - that's 4x the memory and compute of a single model, plus the pipeline needs to actually *generate* text on-policy at every step, which is slow.

It's also finicky: PPO has several sensitive hyperparameters (KL coefficient, clip range, GAE lambda), and reward model gaming/hacking is a constant risk - the policy finds ways to score well on the reward model's proxy metric without genuinely being better (repetitive filler that the reward model happens to over-score, for instance).

DPO's insight was that you don't need any of this. It mathematically shows that the RLHF objective has a closed-form optimal policy in terms of the reward function, which lets you substitute the reward model entirely and rewrite the loss directly in terms of the policy model's own log-probabilities on chosen vs rejected responses - no reward model, no PPO rollout loop, no critic. It's just supervised-learning-style training on preference pairs.

Interview takeaway: DPO removes an entire training stage (reward modeling) and an entire unstable algorithm (PPO) by re-deriving the same optimization target in closed form - simpler, cheaper, and empirically competitive.

Explain the actual DPO loss function intuition - what is it pushing the model to do, mathematically speaking?

DPO's loss looks at, for each preference pair, how much more probability the current policy assigns to the chosen response versus the rejected response, *relative to* how much more probability the frozen reference (SFT) model assigned to the same pair - then pushes that ratio up via a sigmoid-based loss (borrowed from the Bradley-Terry preference model).

Concretely: it computes log π(chosen)/π_ref(chosen) minus log π(rejected)/π_ref(rejected), and trains the policy to make this difference large and positive. Because both terms are normalized against the same frozen reference model, the loss implicitly defines a reward without ever training an explicit reward model - that's the 'implicit reward' framing DPO papers talk about.

The reference-model normalization is what prevents the policy from just uniformly increasing the probability of anything labeled 'chosen' regardless of context - it specifically rewards moving probability mass *away from what the base model already believed* toward the preferred answer, and penalizes moving toward the rejected one, which keeps training stable and prevents mode collapse into always producing one generic 'safe' response.

Interview takeaway: DPO is preference classification in disguise - it's fitting a classifier that says 'chosen beats rejected' by adjusting the policy's own relative log-probabilities, with the frozen reference model anchoring the scale.

What is the 'alignment tax', and have you seen it in your own SFT to DPO comparison?

The alignment tax refers to the observation that aligning a model more strongly to human preferences (via RLHF or DPO) can measurably reduce performance on some general capability benchmarks (reasoning, factual QA, sometimes raw creativity) even as it improves helpfulness, safety, and preference-win-rate against human judges. The model gets better at being liked and safer to deploy, at a small cost to being maximally capable on every academic metric.

The usual explanation is that alignment training pulls the output distribution toward a narrower, more cautious, more format-consistent region of response-space - which is exactly what you want for a customer-facing assistant, but it can clip some of the raw exploratory capability that scored well on open-ended benchmarks.

In a small-scale domain project (like an SFT-then-DPO run on a 1-2B model), the tax typically shows up subtly: slightly less variety in phrasing, more consistent refusal/hedging patterns, and responses that converge toward the 'chosen' style in the preference data even for questions not directly covered by it - which is exactly the intended generalization, but worth checking it hasn't over-corrected into blandness by spot-checking outputs on prompts outside the DPO dataset's direct coverage.

Interview takeaway: alignment tax is the reminder that DPO/RLHF optimizes for preference, not raw capability - always evaluate both before declaring a DPO run a pure win.

What is Constitutional AI / RLAIF, and why would a company use AI feedback instead of human feedback to align a model?

Constitutional AI (Anthropic's approach) and the broader RLAIF (Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback) family replace some or all of the human preference-labeling step with an AI model judging outputs against a written 'constitution' - a set of principles like 'prefer the response that is more helpful and less harmful.' The base model (or a separate judge model) critiques and revises its own outputs according to those principles, generating the preference pairs that DPO/RLHF then trains on.

The motivation is scale and cost: human preference labeling is slow, expensive, and hard to make consistent across thousands of annotators, while an AI judge can generate preference labels for millions of examples cheaply and with more consistent criteria (assuming the judge itself is well-calibrated). It also makes the alignment criteria explicit and auditable - the 'constitution' is a document you can read and debate, rather than an implicit pattern buried in scattered human ratings.

The obvious risk is circularity: if the judge model has its own biases or blind spots, RLAIF can bake those in at scale faster than human feedback would - which is why most production pipelines use RLAIF for volume and cheap iteration, with a smaller human-labeled set for calibration and final validation.

Interview takeaway: RLAIF trades labeling cost and consistency for a new dependency on judge-model quality - it's a scaling technique, not a replacement for human judgment entirely.

Dense embeddings vs BM25/sparse retrieval - what's actually different, and why do production RAG systems often use both (hybrid search)?

BM25 (sparse retrieval) scores documents by exact term overlap with the query, weighted by term rarity (TF-IDF-style) - it's decades-old, needs no training, and is extremely strong at exact keyword and jargon matching: product SKUs, error codes, legal citations, or a rare technical term will be found reliably because BM25 doesn't need to 'understand' them, just match them.

Dense retrieval encodes both query and documents into vectors from a neural embedding model and ranks by vector similarity - it captures *semantic* similarity, so a query for 'how do I get my money back' can retrieve a chunk about 'refund policy' even with zero shared words. Its weakness is exactly BM25's strength: rare tokens, IDs, and precise terminology can get semantically 'smoothed over' and under-retrieved.

Hybrid search runs both retrievers and merges the results (commonly via Reciprocal Rank Fusion), getting BM25's precision on exact terms and dense retrieval's recall on paraphrased or conceptual queries - which is why most serious production RAG systems (and vector DBs like Qdrant and Weaviate with built-in hybrid support) don't pick one, they combine both and let fusion sort out the ranking.

Interview takeaway: BM25 is 'find the words'; dense retrieval is 'find the meaning' - hybrid search is the practical answer to 'why not both.'

What is Reciprocal Rank Fusion and how does it combine results from BM25 and dense retrieval into one ranked list?

RRF is a simple, training-free way to merge multiple ranked lists (say, BM25's top 20 and dense retrieval's top 20) into a single fused ranking, without needing the two systems' raw scores to be on comparable scales - which matters because BM25 scores and cosine similarity scores are not directly comparable numbers.

For each document, RRF computes a score of 1 / (k + rank) in each list it appears in (k is a small constant, often 60, that dampens the impact of very top ranks), and sums that score across all the lists the document appeared in. A document ranked #1 by BM25 and #3 by dense retrieval gets a healthy combined score even though it wasn't #1 everywhere; a document that only one retriever found still gets counted, but with less weight than one both retrievers agree on.

Its appeal is exactly that it only needs *rank position*, not calibrated scores - which sidesteps a genuinely hard problem (score normalization across different retrieval methods) with a formula simple enough to implement in a few lines, and it's the default fusion method in most hybrid search implementations (Qdrant, Elasticsearch, Azure AI Search).

Interview takeaway: RRF sidesteps score-calibration entirely by fusing on rank, not raw score - simple, robust, and the reason it's the default choice for combining retrievers in production.

What is HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings), and why would generating a fake answer before retrieving actually improve RAG quality?

HyDE flips the usual retrieval step: instead of embedding the user's raw question and searching for similar chunks, you first ask the LLM to generate a *hypothetical answer* to the question (which may be wrong or incomplete - that's fine), then embed *that hypothetical answer* and use it to search the vector store.

The reasoning is that queries and answers often live in different regions of embedding space - a short question ('what is the notice period for resignation?') and the document chunk that actually answers it ('employees must provide 30 days written notice...') can be less similar in embedding space than two *answer-shaped* pieces of text would be to each other. By generating a plausible answer first, you're searching with something that's already shaped like the document you're trying to find, which tends to retrieve more relevant chunks even when the hypothetical answer contains factual errors - the retrieval only needs it to be topically and structurally similar, not correct.

The tradeoff is an extra LLM call before retrieval even starts, adding latency and cost, so it's typically reserved for cases where standard query embedding demonstrably underperforms (short, vague, or jargon-light queries against dense technical documents).

Interview takeaway: HyDE improves retrieval by matching 'answer-shape to answer-shape' instead of 'question-shape to answer-shape' - a clever embedding-space trick, not a factual-accuracy trick.

What is semantic caching, and how does it cut LLM cost and latency beyond simple exact-match caching?

Exact-match caching only helps when the *identical* prompt is sent twice - useful for literal duplicate requests, but useless the moment a user rephrases the same question ('how do I reset my password' vs 'password reset steps'), which is extremely common in real traffic. Semantic caching fixes this by embedding incoming queries and checking similarity against previously cached (query, response) pairs - if a new query's embedding is close enough (above a similarity threshold) to a cached one, you serve the cached response instantly instead of calling the LLM again.

This can meaningfully cut cost and latency in high-traffic, repetitive-query domains like customer support or FAQ assistants, where many users ask semantically identical questions in different words. The engineering challenge is picking the similarity threshold carefully - too loose and you serve wrong answers to subtly different questions (a real correctness risk, not just an inefficiency), too strict and the cache rarely hits, providing little benefit.

Most implementations also cache with a TTL and invalidate on underlying data changes, since a cached answer about a policy that just changed is worse than no cache at all.

Interview takeaway: semantic caching trades a small, tunable correctness risk (threshold errors) for large latency/cost wins in repetitive-query workloads - it's most valuable exactly where naive exact-match caching provides almost no benefit.

Why does streaming a response token-by-token matter for a chat product, beyond just feeling faster?

Streaming (via Server-Sent Events or WebSockets) sends tokens to the client as they're generated instead of waiting for the full response to finish, which changes perceived latency dramatically even when total generation time is unchanged - users see the first words within a few hundred milliseconds instead of staring at a blank screen for the several seconds a long response might take to fully generate.

Beyond perception, it enables genuinely better UX patterns: users can start reading and mentally processing the answer before it finishes, they can interrupt/cancel a response that's clearly going in the wrong direction (saving both their time and your compute cost, since you can stop generation server-side on cancel), and it makes long-form generation (a detailed report, a long code file) feel responsive instead of frozen.

On the infrastructure side, streaming also surfaces failures earlier - if generation is going to fail or produce garbage, you often see it in the first few tokens rather than after paying for the full generation and only then discovering the problem.

Interview takeaway: streaming is a perceived-latency and cancellability win, not a throughput win - total tokens generated and total cost are roughly the same, but user experience and the ability to cut losses early both improve substantially.

What does rate limiting and backpressure actually protect against in an LLM-serving system, and how do you implement it without just making users angry?

LLM inference is expensive and GPU capacity is finite and slow to scale (you can't spin up a new GPU in the time it takes to spin up a stateless web server), so without limits a traffic spike or a buggy retry loop from one client can starve GPU throughput for every other user, or blow through a token budget in minutes. Rate limiting caps how many requests (or tokens) a client can send per time window; backpressure is the system's way of signaling 'slow down' rather than silently dropping or queuing requests indefinitely.

Good implementations layer multiple controls: per-API-key request/token quotas, a request queue with a bounded size (reject with a clear 429 and a Retry-After header once full, rather than queuing forever and timing out anyway), and priority tiers so that, say, paying customers or latency-sensitive endpoints get served before batch/background jobs during contention.

The 'without making users angry' part comes from good signaling: clear error codes and retry-after headers so clients can back off gracefully, exposing usage/quota status proactively, and using token-bucket algorithms that allow reasonable bursts rather than a hard, robotic per-second cap.

Interview takeaway: rate limiting protects the system's scarce GPU capacity; backpressure with clear signaling is what keeps that protection from feeling like random failures to your users.

Why does batching requests together improve LLM serving throughput, and what's the catch?

GPUs are throughput machines - running a single request through a model leaves most of the GPU's parallel compute idle because the matrix multiplications are memory-bandwidth bound at small batch sizes. Batching multiple requests' forward passes together lets the same weight matrices be reused across many requests' activations in one pass, dramatically increasing tokens-processed-per-second per GPU, which is why serving frameworks (vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM) are built around continuous/dynamic batching rather than one-request-at-a-time.

The catch is the latency-throughput tradeoff: naive batching waits to accumulate a batch before starting, adding latency to the first request in the batch while it waits for others to arrive, and a single slow/long request in a batch can hold up shorter ones finishing (head-of-line blocking) if batching is static. Modern solutions use continuous batching - new requests join and completed ones leave a running batch at the token level, not request level, so the GPU stays busy without forcing short requests to wait for long ones to fully finish.

Interview takeaway: batching is how you buy throughput with GPUs that are otherwise underutilized on single requests - continuous batching (not naive fixed-batch) is what makes that not cost you unacceptable per-request latency.

What kinds of memory does an AI agent need, and how is 'short-term' different from 'long-term' agent memory in practice?

Short-term memory is essentially the current context window - the conversation so far, recent tool outputs, and the agent's own scratchpad reasoning for the task at hand. It's fast (just tokens in the prompt) but bounded and disappears the moment the session ends or the context window fills up and older turns get truncated or summarized away.

Long-term memory persists across sessions and is usually implemented externally - a vector database storing embedded facts, past conversation summaries, or user preferences that get retrieved (RAG-style) and injected back into the prompt only when relevant to the current query, rather than being kept in context all the time. This is what lets an agent 'remember' that a user prefers concise answers or already told it their project uses PostgreSQL, weeks after that conversation ended.

A further useful split mirrors cognitive science: episodic memory (specific past events/interactions - 'last Tuesday the user asked about X') versus semantic memory (generalized facts distilled from many interactions - 'this user is a backend engineer'). Production agent systems increasingly maintain both, periodically summarizing episodic logs into semantic facts rather than storing everything verbatim forever.

Interview takeaway: short-term memory is context-window-bound and free; long-term memory requires an explicit retrieval architecture (usually a vector store) and a strategy for what's worth persisting versus what should just be forgotten.

What practical mistakes do people make when using a vector store as an agent's long-term memory?

The most common mistake is storing everything verbatim with no curation - every tool call, every intermediate thought, every raw message - which bloats the store, slows retrieval, and actually hurts recall quality because the genuinely important facts get diluted among noise at query time. Good memory systems summarize and selectively persist (what did we learn, decide, or that the user explicitly said to remember), not log everything indiscriminately.

A second mistake is treating memory retrieval like static RAG - always pulling the top-k most similar memories regardless of relevance - without a recency or importance weighting, so a stale fact from months ago can outrank a very relevant recent correction simply because it's more semantically similar to the current query's wording. Production memory systems (e.g. the 'MemGPT'-style approaches) blend similarity, recency, and importance/frequency scores rather than similarity alone.

A third is forgetting that memory can go stale or become wrong (the user changed their preference, a fact was corrected) with no update/invalidation mechanism, silently feeding contradicted information back into future prompts indefinitely.

Interview takeaway: naive 'store everything, retrieve by similarity' vector memory is a common but flawed default - real agent memory needs curation on write and recency/importance-aware ranking on read.

How do you design a tool/function schema so an LLM agent calls it reliably instead of hallucinating bad arguments?

Reliability starts with the schema itself: give the function a precise, unambiguous name (search_orders_by_id, not handle), a description that states exactly when to use it (and, just as important, when not to), and tightly typed parameters with enums for any field that has a fixed set of valid values rather than a free-text string the model might get creative with.

Keep the number of exposed tools manageable and their purposes non-overlapping - if you give an agent 40 similarly-named tools, argument confusion and wrong-tool selection both rise sharply; grouping or hierarchically exposing tools (a router picks a category first, then a specific tool) scales much better than one flat list.

Require the model to justify uncertain or destructive calls before executing them (a reasoning field before the action, or a confirmation step for anything irreversible), and validate every argument server-side regardless of what the schema promised - a JSON Schema and a docstring shape what the model is *likely* to send, they don't guarantee it, so treat tool arguments as untrusted input needing the same validation any API input would need.

Interview takeaway: schema design shapes the model's *tendency* to call correctly; server-side validation is what actually guarantees safety - never skip the second one because the first one looks solid.

ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, and Tree of Thought are all agent 'thinking' patterns. How do they actually differ in how the agent approaches a task?

ReAct interleaves reasoning and acting one step at a time: think, act (call a tool), observe the result, think again - it's reactive and adapts immediately to what each tool call reveals, which makes it well-suited to tasks where you can't predict what you'll find until you look (open-ended research, debugging).

Plan-and-Execute separates the two phases: first generate a full multi-step plan up front, then execute each step (optionally replanning if a step fails or reveals something that invalidates the rest of the plan). This is more token-efficient for well-understood, decomposable tasks because you're not re-reasoning from scratch after every single tool call, but it's worse when the right next step genuinely depends on what earlier steps discover.

Tree of Thought explores *multiple* candidate reasoning paths in parallel (a tree rather than a single chain), evaluating and pruning branches, then picking the most promising path - powerful for problems with a real search space and multiple plausible approaches (puzzle-solving, complex planning), but far more expensive in tokens and latency since you're generating and scoring several parallel trajectories instead of one.

Interview takeaway: ReAct trades efficiency for adaptability, Plan-and-Execute trades adaptability for efficiency, and Tree of Thought trades cost for exploring more of the solution space - pick based on how predictable the task's next step is and how much budget you have.

What is self-consistency (majority voting) as a technique for improving LLM reasoning, and why does it work?

Self-consistency runs the same prompt multiple times with sampling (temperature > 0, so each run can reason differently), then takes the majority answer across the samples instead of trusting a single greedy generation. The intuition is that for reasoning tasks with one correct answer, there are usually many different *correct* reasoning paths that converge on the same answer, but only a few ways to be *wrong* - and those wrong paths tend to disagree with each other rather than all landing on the same incorrect answer.

So if you sample, say, 10 independent chain-of-thought reasoning traces and 7 of them arrive at '42' while the other 3 scatter across different wrong answers, majority voting picks '42' with much higher reliability than any single sample would have had alone - it's essentially an ensemble over the model's own stochastic reasoning variance.

The obvious cost is 10x the inference calls (and latency, unless run in parallel) for one final answer, so it's reserved for cases where correctness matters more than cost/latency - math and logic benchmarks, high-stakes classification decisions - not for everyday chat responses.

Interview takeaway: self-consistency exploits the fact that correct reasoning paths tend to agree while wrong ones tend to disagree - it buys reliability by trading away a multiple of inference cost.

What's the difference between input-side guardrails and output-side guardrails in a production LLM app, and why do you need both?

Input-side guardrails inspect what goes *into* the model before generation: detecting prompt injection attempts, PII in the user's message, off-topic or malicious requests, and jailbreak patterns - the goal is to stop bad input from ever reaching the model, or to strip/flag it first. This layer protects the model itself from being manipulated and protects downstream systems from receiving obviously malicious instructions.

Output-side guardrails inspect what the model *produces* before it reaches the user or a downstream system: checking for hallucinated claims against source documents, PII leakage, toxic or off-brand content, and schema/format validation for structured outputs feeding another system. This layer catches problems that originate from the model itself, not just from bad input - a model can still hallucinate or go off-policy even given a perfectly clean prompt.

You need both because they catch different failure classes: input guardrails alone won't stop a model from hallucinating a wrong fact on a completely innocent question, and output guardrails alone won't stop a cleverly injected instruction from hijacking the model's behavior before generation even starts.

Interview takeaway: input guardrails protect the model from the world; output guardrails protect the world from the model - a serious deployment needs a stack, not a single filter.

How do you handle PII (personally identifiable information) safely in an LLM pipeline, both going in and coming out?

On the way in, PII shows up in user messages, uploaded documents, or retrieved RAG chunks - a common pattern is running a PII detection/redaction step (regex for structured PII like emails and phone numbers, plus an NER model for names and addresses that don't follow a fixed pattern) before that content is logged, embedded into a vector store, or sent to a third-party model API, especially if that API's data-retention policy is uncertain.

On the way out, the model can *generate* PII it shouldn't - either by leaking something present in its context (a retrieved document containing another user's data) or, more subtly, by hallucinating plausible-looking but fake PII (a fabricated phone number or SSN-shaped string) that looks authoritative even though it's fiction, which is its own kind of harm. Output-side scanning and redaction before the response is shown or logged catches both cases.

Beyond redaction, the higher-leverage fix is architectural: don't let the model see more PII than the current task needs (scope retrieval and context tightly), and treat any logs, caches, or fine-tuning datasets built from real user interactions as needing the same PII handling as the live pipeline - a vector store full of unredacted support tickets is a compliance liability months after the original conversations.

Interview takeaway: PII handling isn't just an input filter - it has to cover retrieval, generation, and storage/logging, because a leak can happen at any of those three points.

What is data contamination in LLM benchmarks, and why should you be skeptical of a model that scores unusually well on a popular benchmark?

Data contamination happens when benchmark test questions (or very close paraphrases) leak into a model's pretraining or fine-tuning corpus - given the internet-scale scraping most base models are trained on, and the fact that popular benchmarks like MMLU or GSM8K have been online, discussed, and copied for years, some contamination is almost unavoidable at scale unless the training pipeline explicitly filters for it.

A contaminated model isn't actually more capable - it's effectively memorized answers to specific questions it will be tested on, which inflates its benchmark score without reflecting genuine generalization to novel problems of the same type. This is exactly why a model can top a leaderboard yet disappoint in a real production use case that superficially resembles the benchmark but isn't literally in it.

Signs worth being skeptical of: a smaller/cheaper model suspiciously outscoring much larger, well-known models on one specific benchmark while performing unremarkably on others measuring similar skills; a big score jump on a benchmark right after a training run with no architectural change to explain it; and any lack of transparency from the model provider about contamination-filtering methodology.

Interview takeaway: benchmark scores are only trustworthy alongside contamination-checking methodology - when evaluating a model for a real project, always validate on your own held-out, private test set, not just published leaderboard numbers.

An agent has a Search tool. It searches, misreads the result, and confidently calls a different tool with the wrong arguments derived from that misread. How do you mitigate this kind of chained hallucination in agent tool use?

This is the agent equivalent of hallucination compounding: an early reasoning error becomes an input to a later step, and because tool calls look structured and confident, the error doesn't 'feel' uncertain the way a hedged natural-language guess would - it just becomes a wrong function call.

Mitigation starts with making intermediate reasoning visible and checkable: require the agent to explicitly state what it extracted from a tool result *before* using it in the next call (a verification/reflection step), which both improves accuracy (forcing explicit extraction reduces silent misreading) and gives you a log to debug when something goes wrong. Second, validate tool outputs are being used correctly with lightweight sanity checks - if a 'price' argument extracted from a search result is a string like 'not found' instead of a number, catch that before the next tool call fires rather than letting it propagate.

Third, add self-correction loops: after a chain of tool calls, have the agent (or a separate checker call) re-verify the final answer against the original tool outputs, which catches drift that accumulated across multiple steps. Finally, cap the blast radius - rate-limit tool calls per task, and require human confirmation before any step with real-world side effects (sending something, modifying a record, spending money), so a hallucination chain can be caught before it causes damage rather than only after.

Interview takeaway: chained hallucination compounds because each step trusts the last one blindly - the fix is making every intermediate extraction explicit and checkable, not just trusting structured-looking tool calls.

When a conversation gets too long for the context window, what are the actual strategies for managing that, beyond just truncating old messages?

Hard truncation (dropping the oldest messages) is the simplest strategy but loses information the user might reference later ('like you said earlier...') and can cut off mid-context in a way that confuses the model about what's already been established.

A better default is rolling summarization: periodically (every N turns, or when approaching a token threshold) replace the oldest chunk of conversation with an LLM-generated summary of it, preserving the gist - key facts, decisions, user preferences - while freeing up most of the tokens those turns consumed. This is what most production chat products do under the hood once a conversation gets long.

For agentic tasks specifically, a scratchpad/working-memory pattern helps: keep only the current task's relevant state (not the full raw history) in context, and offload anything else to external memory (a vector store or structured state) that gets retrieved only when relevant, rather than kept in the window at all times.

A fourth option for retrieval-heavy conversations is hybrid: keep the last few turns verbatim (recency matters for conversational coherence) plus a summary of everything older, plus RAG-retrieved facts relevant to the current turn specifically - giving the model recent detail, long-range gist, and on-demand specifics without ever needing the full raw transcript.

Interview takeaway: naive truncation loses information silently; summarization and external memory trade a little fidelity for graceful degradation as conversations grow, which is what real products need.

Fixed-size chunking just splits text every N tokens. What is semantic chunking, and when is the extra complexity worth it?

Fixed-size chunking (say, every 500 tokens with some overlap) is simple, fast, and predictable, but it's blind to meaning - it will happily slice a sentence, a table, or a single coherent idea right down the middle, which directly hurts retrieval quality because the resulting chunk may be missing the context needed to make sense of it, or match a query on a fragment without the accompanying explanation.

Semantic chunking instead splits text at natural meaning boundaries - typically by embedding consecutive sentences and cutting where the embedding similarity between adjacent sentences drops sharply (a topic shift), or by respecting document structure (headings, paragraphs, list boundaries) so each chunk is a self-contained, coherent unit of meaning rather than an arbitrary token-count slice.

The extra complexity (an embedding pass over the document just to decide where to cut, plus more careful engineering) is worth it for dense, information-rich documents where chunk coherence directly drives answer quality - legal contracts, technical manuals, medical documents - where a badly-split chunk can genuinely produce a wrong or incomplete answer. For loosely structured, redundant text (casual FAQ pages, forum posts) the gains are smaller and fixed-size chunking is often good enough.

Interview takeaway: fixed-size chunking optimizes for engineering simplicity; semantic chunking optimizes for retrieval quality on structurally important documents - choose based on how much a badly-cut chunk would actually hurt answer correctness in your domain.

Bigger embedding dimensionality generally captures more nuance. So why don't production RAG systems just always use the largest embedding model available?

Higher-dimensional embeddings do generally encode more semantic nuance, but the gains are not free and often not linear - a 3072-dimension embedding isn't necessarily proportionally better than a 1024-dimension one for a given retrieval task, especially once the corpus and query complexity are modest.

The real costs scale directly with dimensionality: storage (a million documents at 3072 floats each is several times larger than at 768), index build and query latency (nearest-neighbor search over higher-dimensional vectors is slower, especially for exact search and even for approximate methods like HNSW), and the cost of the embedding calls themselves if using a hosted API charged per token or per call.

In practice, teams benchmark embedding models on their *actual* retrieval task (using metrics like recall@k on a labeled query-document set) rather than assuming bigger wins, and many find a well-chosen mid-sized embedding model (or even a fine-tuned smaller one for their domain vocabulary) performs comparably to a much larger general-purpose one at a fraction of the storage and latency cost - domain-specific fine-tuning of a smaller embedding model can outperform a bigger generic one entirely.

Interview takeaway: embedding dimensionality is a capacity/cost tradeoff like any other model size decision - always benchmark on your actual retrieval task rather than assuming the largest available embedding model is automatically the right choice.

INT8, INT4, GPTQ, AWQ - there are several quantization approaches for shrinking LLMs. How do they actually differ?

INT8 and INT4 describe the *target precision* - how many bits represent each weight after quantization (8-bit roughly halves memory vs fp16, 4-bit roughly quarters it) - but not *how* the conversion from higher precision is done, which is where GPTQ and AWQ come in as specific quantization *algorithms* that can target either bit-width.

GPTQ (Generative Pre-trained Transformer Quantization) is a post-training quantization method that quantizes weights layer by layer, using a small calibration dataset and second-order (Hessian-based) error correction to adjust remaining weights and compensate for the error introduced by quantizing each one - it's accurate but the calibration process takes real compute time to run once per model.

AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantization) takes a different angle: it observes that not all weights matter equally - a small fraction of weights, corresponding to high-magnitude activations, disproportionately affect output quality - and protects those salient weights with less aggressive quantization (or scaling) while quantizing the rest harder, often achieving comparable or better quality than GPTQ with a simpler, faster calibration process.

Interview takeaway: bit-width (INT4/INT8) is 'how small'; GPTQ/AWQ are 'how carefully' - GPTQ leans on Hessian-based error correction, AWQ leans on protecting activation-salient weights, and both usually beat naive uniform rounding by a wide margin at the same bit-width.

FlashAttention is mentioned everywhere as a training/inference speedup. In plain terms, what problem does it solve?

The mathematical operation of attention hasn't changed with FlashAttention - it computes the exact same softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d))V result. What changed is *how* that computation moves data around the GPU's memory hierarchy, which turns out to be the actual bottleneck, not the raw FLOPs.

Standard attention implementations materialize the full n x n attention score matrix in GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM) - reading and writing that large intermediate matrix repeatedly is slow because HBM, while large, has much lower bandwidth than the GPU's on-chip SRAM. FlashAttention restructures the computation into tiles: it processes blocks of the Q, K, V matrices that fit in fast SRAM, computes partial softmax results incrementally (using a running-max trick to keep the softmax numerically stable without seeing the whole row at once), and never writes the full attention matrix to slow memory at all.

The result is an exact (not approximate) computation that's significantly faster and uses less memory, which is why it enabled both faster training and, crucially, much longer context windows than were previously practical - memory that used to be spent materializing giant attention matrices is now available for longer sequences instead.

Interview takeaway: FlashAttention is an I/O-aware reimplementation, not a new algorithm - same math, dramatically less data movement between slow and fast GPU memory, which is exactly where the real bottleneck was.

What is gradient checkpointing, and why would you deliberately choose to make training slower to use it?

During a normal forward pass, the model caches every intermediate activation because they're needed to compute gradients during backpropagation - for a deep model with a long sequence, that adds up to enormous memory usage, often becoming the actual limiting factor on batch size or sequence length far before compute does.

Gradient checkpointing (activation checkpointing) trades compute for memory: instead of storing every intermediate activation, it stores only a subset (checkpoints, e.g. at each transformer layer boundary) and discards the rest, then during the backward pass it *recomputes* the discarded activations on the fly from the nearest checkpoint by re-running that portion of the forward pass. This roughly cuts activation memory dramatically at the cost of a noticeable slowdown (commonly cited around 20-30% more compute time) from doing some forward computation twice.

The reason you'd accept that: memory, not compute, is usually the wall you hit first on a single GPU - a bigger batch size or longer sequence that gradient checkpointing enables can improve training stability and throughput enough to more than make up for the per-step slowdown, and on constrained hardware (a free Colab GPU) it's often the difference between a fine-tuning job fitting in memory at all versus crashing with OOM.

Interview takeaway: gradient checkpointing is a deliberate compute-for-memory trade - slower per step, but it can be the only way to fit a larger batch or longer sequence on limited GPU memory, which is exactly why Unsloth and PEFT enable it by default for LoRA/QLoRA training.

Why train in mixed precision (fp16/bf16) instead of full fp32, and what's the practical difference between fp16 and bf16?

fp32 gives the most numerical precision but at double the memory and roughly half the throughput of 16-bit formats on modern GPU tensor cores, which are specifically optimized for 16-bit matrix multiplication - so mixed precision training keeps most operations in a 16-bit format for speed and memory savings, while selectively keeping precision-sensitive operations (like the master weight copy and certain reductions) in fp32 to maintain training stability.

fp16 and bf16 split those 16 bits differently: fp16 gives more mantissa bits (more precision) but far fewer exponent bits (a much smaller representable range), which makes it prone to overflow/underflow during training - very large or very small gradient values can literally become inf or 0, which is why fp16 training traditionally needs loss scaling (multiplying the loss by a large constant before backprop, then dividing gradients back down) as a workaround. bf16 keeps the same exponent range as fp32 (so it handles the same range of magnitudes without overflow) but with fewer mantissa bits, trading some precision for much better numerical stability - which is why bf16 has become the default choice on hardware that supports it (A100s, H100s, and importantly the newer consumer/Colab GPUs), needing no loss scaling at all.

Interview takeaway: mixed precision trains faster and lighter than fp32 on modern tensor cores; bf16 is generally preferred over fp16 today because its wider exponent range avoids the overflow/underflow instability that made fp16 finicky.

MMLU, HellaSwag, and similar benchmarks are everywhere in model announcements. Why aren't they enough to evaluate a GenAI product built on top of a model?

These benchmarks measure fairly narrow, static capabilities - multiple-choice academic knowledge (MMLU), commonsense sentence completion (HellaSwag) - in a format (usually multiple choice, single correct answer, no conversation) that looks almost nothing like how a real product uses the model: multi-turn conversations, open-ended generation, tool use, retrieval-augmented answers, domain-specific jargon, and success criteria like 'was this actually helpful' rather than 'was option C selected.'

A model can score well on MMLU and still be a mediocre customer support assistant, because MMLU says nothing about tone, adherence to your company's policies, hallucination rate on your specific documents, latency under your traffic patterns, or how it behaves with your actual users' messy, ambiguous phrasing - none of which the benchmark ever tested.

That's why serious GenAI evaluation for a product layers task-specific evaluation on top: a held-out set of real (or realistic) queries from your domain, human or LLM-as-judge scoring against criteria that matter to your use case (correctness, helpfulness, safety, tone), and production monitoring of real user feedback/behavior signals - academic benchmarks are a reasonable signal for comparing base model capability, not a substitute for evaluating your actual application.

Interview takeaway: academic benchmarks answer 'how capable is this model in general'; product evaluation must answer 'does this model do my specific job well' - they're related but not interchangeable, and only the second one tells you if you should ship.

How would you A/B test a change to an LLM prompt or a model swap in production, when the 'right answer' for a given query isn't a single fixed string?

Unlike a button-color A/B test with a clean binary outcome, LLM changes need proxy metrics because there's rarely one objectively correct response - so you pick metrics that correlate with quality and are measurable at scale: user behavior signals (thumbs up/down, whether the user rephrased and re-asked, session abandonment, whether a follow-up 'that's wrong' type message occurred), task-completion signals if applicable (did the conversation lead to a resolved ticket, a completed purchase), and periodic LLM-as-judge or human-rated scoring on a sampled subset for qualitative dimensions that behavioral signals miss.

Mechanically, you route a percentage of real traffic to the new prompt/model (variant B) while the rest stays on the current version (control A), keeping the split randomized and stable per-user (not per-request, so a single user gets consistent behavior within a session) and running long enough to reach statistical confidence given your traffic volume - low-traffic domains may need weeks, not hours.

Critically, track cost and latency alongside quality metrics, not quality in isolation - a prompt change that improves the judge score by 2% but doubles token usage or adds 3 seconds of latency may not be a net win depending on the product's priorities.

Interview takeaway: LLM A/B testing swaps a single ground-truth metric for a bundle of proxy signals (behavioral + sampled quality scoring) precisely because there's no single correct string to compare against - design the metric bundle before you design the experiment.

What is shadow deployment, and why would you use it before an A/B test when rolling out a new LLM model version?

Shadow deployment (or 'dark launch') runs the new model or prompt version on real production traffic *in parallel* with the current live version, but its output is never actually shown to users - it's logged and compared against the live version's output purely for internal evaluation. The user only ever sees the existing, already-validated version's response.

This matters as a step before A/B testing because it lets you catch obviously broken behavior - crashes, wildly off-topic responses, format violations, safety issues - on real, messy production traffic (which is always weirder and more varied than any test set you curated) with zero user-facing risk, since nobody ever sees the shadow output. Only after a shadow run looks healthy (no crashes, reasonable outputs on a sample review, acceptable latency) do you graduate to a real A/B test where a fraction of users actually receive the new version's responses and you can measure genuine behavioral/quality impact.

The limitation is that shadow deployment can't measure some things that require actual user reaction - you can't shadow-test whether a friendlier tone increases user satisfaction, because satisfaction requires a real user experiencing it - so it's a safety gate for catching breakage, not a substitute for the A/B test that actually measures quality impact.

Interview takeaway: shadow deployment de-risks a rollout against production-scale surprises before you ever expose users to it; A/B testing is the separate, subsequent step that actually measures whether the change is better.

How does context length affect the actual dollar cost of an LLM API call, and what's a concrete lever to reduce it without losing quality?

Most hosted LLM APIs charge per token, for both input (prompt) and output tokens, and a long system prompt, several retrieved RAG chunks, and conversation history all count as input tokens on *every single call* - so a chatty conversation with a large system prompt and generous RAG context can cost many times more per turn than a short, stateless single-question API call, even though the user only sees one short exchange.

A concrete, high-leverage lever is trimming what's re-sent every turn: cache and reuse a summarized conversation history instead of the full raw transcript (see the earlier point on managing long conversations), retrieve fewer but higher-precision RAG chunks (better re-ranking beats sending 10 mediocre chunks 'just in case'), and move static, rarely-changing instructions into a place that benefits from prompt caching if the provider supports it (many APIs now offer discounted pricing for a cached, unchanged prefix of the prompt across repeated calls, which a stable system prompt is a perfect candidate for).

Another lever, model routing, sends easy/short queries to a cheaper, smaller model and reserves the expensive frontier model for genuinely hard queries - since not every user question needs the biggest model's capability, and the classification of 'easy vs hard' can itself be done cheaply.

Interview takeaway: cost scales with tokens sent on every call, not just output length - the highest-leverage savings usually come from shrinking what you re-send every turn (context, history, RAG chunks), not from switching to a marginally cheaper model.

How does LangGraph model an agent as a state machine - what are nodes, edges, and checkpointing actually doing?

LangGraph represents an agent's workflow as a graph: nodes are units of work (a call to the LLM, a tool invocation, a piece of custom logic), and edges define the control flow between them - including *conditional* edges, where the next node is chosen dynamically based on the current state (e.g., 'if the LLM decided to call a tool, go to the tool node; otherwise go to the end'). The shared 'state' object flows through the graph, getting read and updated by each node, which makes the whole execution path explicit and inspectable rather than hidden inside an opaque agent loop.

This state-machine framing is what lets LangGraph support things a simple loop struggles with cleanly: cycles (an agent revisiting a node multiple times, like retrying a tool call), branching (different paths for different outcomes), and multiple cooperating nodes representing different agents or roles in a larger workflow.

Checkpointing persists the state at each step to storage, which enables pausing a long-running agent workflow and resuming it later (including after a crash), replaying execution from any earlier point for debugging, and - importantly for production agents that need a human in the loop - stopping at a specific node to wait for human approval before continuing, rather than needing to keep the whole process alive in memory the entire time.

Interview takeaway: LangGraph trades a simple, implicit agent loop for an explicit, inspectable state machine - the complexity is worth it exactly when you need cycles, branching, multi-agent coordination, or durable pause/resume, which a flat ReAct loop can't cleanly express.

CrewAI structures multi-agent systems around roles, tasks, and crews. What does that abstraction actually buy you over just writing custom orchestration code?

CrewAI's core abstraction assigns each agent a role (a persona and area of responsibility, like 'Senior Researcher' or 'Content Editor', complete with a goal and backstory that shapes its system prompt), a set of tasks (specific units of work with expected outputs, which can depend on other tasks' outputs), and groups agents into a crew that executes tasks either sequentially or with a hierarchical manager agent delegating work.

What this buys you over hand-rolled orchestration is a ready-made vocabulary and execution engine for a very common pattern - specialized agents collaborating on a multi-step deliverable - so you get task dependency handling, role-consistent prompting, and inter-agent context passing without writing that plumbing yourself for every new multi-agent project. It's opinionated toward role-based collaboration (think of it as modeling a small team with defined job descriptions) rather than the more general, graph-based flexibility of something like LangGraph.

The tradeoff is exactly that opinionation: CrewAI is fast to get a role-based multi-agent workflow running, but if your workflow needs complex conditional branching, cycles, or fine-grained control over execution flow, you'll find yourself fighting the role/task/crew abstraction rather than being helped by it - at which point a more flexible graph-based framework is the better fit.

Interview takeaway: CrewAI optimizes for fast-to-build, role-based collaborative agent teams; it trades away the fine-grained control that graph-based frameworks like LangGraph offer for more complex or cyclical workflows.
